1.synthesis: The first formal lament over Pharaoh (March 3, 585 BCE—approximately six months after Jerusalem’s fall in the summer of 586 BCE) opens the final sustained treatment of Egypt in Ezekiel. YHWH instructs Ezekiel to “take up a lamentation for Pharaoh king of Egypt and say to him”—the qinah directed personally at the king. The opening image is double: Pharaoh thought of himself as a young lion among nations, but he is actually a sea monster (tannin) in the seas, churning the waters, fouling them with his feet and muddying the rivers (v. 2). The lion image is Egypt’s royal iconography (the sphinx); the tannin echoes the chaos creature of 29:3. YHWH the divine fisherman casts his net, hauls Pharaoh onto dry land, scatters his flesh on the mountains and fills the valleys with his remains. The cosmic consequences are extraordinary: “when I blot you out I will cover the heavens and darken their stars; I will cover the sun with a cloud and the moon will not give its light” (v. 7)—Egypt’s fall triggers cosmic darkness, the undoing of the luminaries YHWH set in place at creation. After the mythological imagery, the oracle specifies the human instrument: “the sword of the king of Babylon will come against you” (v. 11), and the qinah closes with the formal identification: “this is the lamentation they will sing over her” (v. 16).
1.historical_context: The date March 3, 585 BCE places this oracle after Jerusalem’s fall in August/September 586 BCE. Pharaoh Hophra (Apries) remained on Egypt’s throne until approximately 570 BCE. The cosmic darkness imagery of vv. 7–8 is the most extensive in the prophetic literature: sun, moon, stars, and “all the luminous lights of the sky” darkened. This draws on the theophany tradition where darkness accompanies YHWH’s decisive acts (Exodus 10:21–23, the plague of darkness in Egypt; Joel 2:10; Amos 8:9). The “daughters of the nations” who sing the lament (v. 16) are the female mourners of the surrounding peoples—a professional lamenting corps who would sing formal qinot at major deaths. Their singing the lament over Egypt signals that Egypt’s fall is a geopolitical event of the first order, lamented across the entire region.
1.literary_structure: The lament spans sixteen verses in three movements: the lion/tannin image and divine fishing (vv. 1–6), the cosmic darkness and its effects on the nations (vv. 7–10), and the specification of Babylon as agent and the city/livestock destruction (vv. 11–15), closed by the formal identification of the oracle as a qinah (v. 16). The shift from mythological imagery (tannin, cosmic darkness) to specific military detail (Babylon’s sword, vv. 11–12) is characteristic of Ezekiel’s technique: the cosmic frame interprets the historical event, and the historical event grounds the cosmic frame. The qinah form that opened with “take up a lamentation” concludes with the identification of who will sing it, creating an envelope structure around the entire oracle.
1.theological_application: The cosmic darkness that accompanies Egypt’s fall is a theological statement about the interdependence of human pride and the created order. When a civilization organized around the sun (Pharaoh as son of Re, Egypt as the gift of the Nile’s waters) is judged, even the sun darkens. This is not literal astronomical change but the prophetic insistence that human sin and divine judgment operate at a scale that implicates all of creation. Paul’s statement that “the creation was subjected to futility, not willingly, but because of him who subjected it” (Romans 8:20) resonates with this oracle: the darkening of Egypt’s sun is creation groaning under the weight of human rebellion and its consequences. Egypt’s solar theology made it uniquely vulnerable to this darkening: what you trust becomes what fails you most completely.
1.christology: The cosmic darkness at Egypt’s fall in Ezekiel’s qinah is replicated at the darkest moment of the New Testament: “from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour” (Matthew 27:45). The darkness at the cross is the darkness of Ezekiel’s Egypt oracle enacted upon the Son of God: the judgment that was to fall on empire falls on Christ in their place. But where Egypt’s darkness signals the permanent end of its solar-theological system, the darkness at Calvary lasts three hours before giving way to the three days that end in resurrection light. The sun that does not shine at Egypt’s fall becomes the sun of righteousness that rises at Christ’s resurrection (Malachi 4:2).

17.synthesis: The verse of Ezekiel 32:17—“In the twelfth year, on the fifteenth of the month, the word of the LORD came to me”—opens the second and final lament over Egypt with a date formula placed two weeks after the first lament. March 17, 585 BCE marks the formal opening of the Sheol-descent oracle, which is both the most extended underworld vision in the Hebrew Bible and the culminating oracle of the entire Egypt cycle. The significance of this two-week interval between the laments (March 3 and March 17) is not explicitly theological but structural: Ezekiel receives two distinct oracles about Egypt in the same month, suggesting an intensity of prophetic attention to Egypt’s fate in the months following Jerusalem’s fall. The word-event formula marks the boundary between the qinah over Pharaoh personally (vv. 1–16) and the broader wail over “Egypt’s multitude” (vv. 18–32)—the shift from the king to the nation and its military power. This single verse thus carries the structural weight of a paragraph heading: it marks a new divine speech-event, received on a new day, introducing a new and expanded oracle that what preceded it.
17.historical_context: The twelfth year of Jehoiachin’s captivity (the dating system Ezekiel uses throughout) equals 585 BCE, placing this oracle approximately six to seven months after Jerusalem’s fall. The two-lament structure of ch. 32 (March 3 and March 17) may reflect a liturgical or editorial decision: the two oracles form a diptych, the first focused on Pharaoh the individual ruler (as the tannin image suggests) and the second on Egypt as a civilization descending to the underworld with its military multitude. The date formula without a specified month (“fifteenth of the month”) has led some commentators to question the text; the Septuagint reads “in the first month,” which would place this on April 1, 585 BCE. The uncertainty does not affect the oracle’s theological content. The precision of dating throughout Ezekiel reflects the prophetic tradition of grounding divine speech in historical time.
17.literary_structure: As a single date-formula verse, v. 17 is structurally parallel to the date formula of v. 1 that opened the first lament. The parallelism signals that the reader is encountering a second, distinct oracle unit within the same chapter. Ezekiel’s editorial arrangement frequently places two oracles on related subjects within a single chapter (cf. the two Tyre oracles in ch. 28: vv. 1–10 and vv. 11–19; the two Israel-restoration promises in ch. 36: vv. 1–15 and vv. 16–38). In each case, the second oracle deepens or extends the first. Here, where the first lament described Pharaoh’s fall in metaphorical terms (tannin, cosmic darkness), the second lament will describe Egypt’s actual underworld assembly with the nations who preceded it—moving from poetic image to visionary reality.
17.theological_application: The division of a single chapter into two dated oracle-units communicates that YHWH’s word does not exhaust itself in one utterance: there is always more to be said, a deeper angle from which to view the same reality. The first lament shows how Egypt’s king falls; the second shows where all nations go when they fall—the underworld assembly that awaits every empire. Theologically, this models the fullness of prophetic witness: single perspectives on divine judgment are insufficient; the Spirit provides multiple viewpoints on the same event to ensure that no facet of its meaning is missed. Communities that engage scripture as multi-perspectival witness to divine action are enacting the same principle: the same event (Egypt’s fall, the crucifixion, the resurrection) requires multiple accounts because no single perspective captures the whole.
17.christology: The two-lament structure of ch. 32 prefigures the two-testament structure of Christian scripture: the first perspective (Law and Prophets) shows the pattern of human pride and divine judgment; the second perspective (New Testament) reveals where that pattern ultimately goes and what divine grace does about it. Just as the second lament of ch. 32 deepens and completes the first by revealing the underworld assembly, the New Testament deepens and completes the Old by revealing the resurrection assembly—where Christ leads captivity captive and presents the redeemed before the Father (Hebrews 2:13; Ephesians 4:8). The word that comes on March 17, 585 BCE has its final answer in the word spoken on Easter morning.

18.synthesis: The second lament of ch. 32 is Ezekiel’s most extended underworld vision—a tour of Sheol in which Egypt is brought down to join the nations that preceded it in violent death. YHWH commands: “Wail for the multitude of Egypt and bring them down—both Egypt and the daughters of the majestic nations—to the depths of the earth, with those who go down to the Pit” (v. 18). The rhetorical question addressed to Egypt is withering: “Whom do you surpass in beauty? Go down and lie with the uncircumcised” (v. 19). Then the roll call of nations already in Sheol: Assyria with all its multitude, graves surrounding it, all slain by the sword (v. 22); Elam with its multitude, carrying terror in the land of the living, now disgraced below (v. 24); Meshech and Tubal, Edom, and the princes of the north and all the Sidonians. Egypt’s descent is the last in a series: it joins an already-populated underworld assembly of the world’s great powers, each lying in the shame of violent death, each stripped of the terror it once spread in the land of the living. The Sheol of Ezekiel 32 is not an abstract afterlife concept but a moral geography: the nations that spread terror have gathered in the place where terror’s pretensions are permanently exposed as empty.
18.historical_context: The underworld assembly of vv. 22–32 is one of the most historically specific visions of Sheol in the Hebrew Bible. Assyria (fallen 612 BCE), Elam (the ancient kingdom of southwestern Iran, disrupted by Ashurbanipal around 640 BCE and subsequently declining), Meshech and Tubal (peoples of central/eastern Anatolia, pressed by Scythian and later Persian advances), Edom, and Sidon are all named as already in the underworld when Egypt descends. The phrase “lying with the uncircumcised” is the oracle’s repeated refrain of shame: in ancient Near Eastern funerary practice, burial rites and bodily integrity marked civilized identity; “uncircumcised” in this context denotes those outside the covenant community who died without proper burial, dishonored in death as they dishonored others in life. The warriors of old who went down with their weapons (v. 27) may refer to the heroic dead of ancient mythology, whose honorable warrior burial contrasts with the dishonored death of these imperial multitudes.
18.literary_structure: The section is structured as a catalog of underworld inhabitants: Egypt is addressed (vv. 18–21), then Assyria (vv. 22–23), Elam (vv. 24–25), Meshech-Tubal (vv. 26–28), Edom (v. 29), Sidon (v. 30), and finally Pharaoh and all his army (vv. 31–32). The recurring refrain “they spread terror in the land of the living” (natatnu chittitam be’eretz chayyim) appears at the close of nearly every entry, creating a liturgical quality: each nation’s former terror is remembered precisely at the moment of its eternal shaming. The final word is YHWH’s: “Pharaoh will see them and be comforted for all his multitude” (v. 31)—the darkest comfort, the only consolation available to the defeated: the knowledge that others fell before them.
18.theological_application: The Sheol-vision of vv. 18–32 is a sustained meditation on the ultimate futility of military terror as a basis for civilizational identity. Each nation in the underworld assembly is identified by the same qualifier: it “spread terror in the land of the living.” This terror was their legacy and their claim to power—and it is precisely this terror that is exposed as hollow in Sheol, where they lie disgraced alongside those they terrorized. The vision insists that power built on terror has no eschatological standing; it ends in the shame of lying with the uncircumcised. The application to every form of coercive power—political, military, economic, social—is clear: the terror of the living becomes the shame of the dead. Only the kind of power that serves rather than terrifies has any claim to lasting significance.
18.christology: The assembly of nations in Sheol—each identified by the terror they spread in the land of the living, each now shamed in the underworld—is the dark image that Christ’s descent and resurrection addresses directly. 1 Peter 3:18–19 speaks of Christ preaching to the spirits in prison; Revelation 1:18 declares he holds the keys of Death and Hades. The nations in Ezekiel’s Sheol-assembly, lying in the shame of their violence, are the precise population over whom Christ declares his lordship. He does not merely join them, as Pharaoh joins Assyria; he enters as the conquering Servant whose death was not the failure of power but the redemptive exercise of a different kind of power entirely. The king of kings enters the assembly of failed kings not to be comforted by their company but to liberate those they enslaved (Colossians 2:15: “he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him”).
